title: Top view of the Binary Tree

content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, print the top view of the tree. The top view of a binary tree is the set of nodes visible when the tree is viewed from the top. It is defined as the nodes visible from the top along the vertical line passing through the leftmost node to rightmost node of the tree.

Examples:




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Print on the single line, KKK (if there are KKK nodes visible from the top) space separated node values - the top view nodes of the given binary tree from left to right.
constraints: All pi's and ci's are distinct
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree. Each of the next N-1 lines contains three space-separated values: pi, ci, and Ri, where pi is the parent node, ci is the child node, and Ri is either 'L' or 'R', indicating whether the child is the left or right child of the parent.
content.outputFormat: Print the top view of the binary tree, which is the set of nodes visible when the tree is viewed from the top, on a single line, separated by spaces.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The top view of the given binary tree is 6 2 1 3 5.

solution.approach: To find the top view of a binary tree, we can use a breadth-first search (BFS) traversal and keep track of the nodes at each horizontal distance from the root. We start by initializing a queue with the root node and its horizontal distance. Then, we perform BFS traversal, and for each node, we check if we have encountered a node at the same horizontal distance before. If not, we add the node's value to the top view. We continue this process until we have traversed all nodes in the tree.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N), where N is the number of nodes in the tree, since we visit each node once during the BFS traversal.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N), since in the worst case, we might need to store all nodes in the queue.
